title: Before You Roll Into FY27: The Commercial Debrief Most Owner-Led Businesses Skip
linkedin:
FY26 is done. The tax return is in the queue. For most Australian business owners, that is where the June 30 conversation ends.

But the most useful review of the year has not happened yet.

Your accountant is doing the right thing: closing the books, preparing the return, meeting the ATO's requirements. That work matters. But the tax return is designed to answer the ATO's question, not yours.

The commercial questions worth asking today are not on a lodgement form.

Where did gross margin land this year, and do you know why? If it compressed during FY26, the cause is almost certainly still active in the business. Knowing whether that compression came from pricing, input costs, or a shift in the revenue mix tells you which lever to reach for in July.

Which revenue lines actually carried the year, and which ones quietly consumed capacity without contributing proportionally? Full-year numbers often reveal a picture that month-by-month reporting obscures.

Did the year's profit convert into cash? A year that ends with more outstanding debtors and less cash than it started with is telling you something important about your working capital cycle.

Each of these questions has an answer sitting in your June 30 numbers right now. The question is whether anyone is looking for it.

Owners who run a structured year-end commercial debrief in the first two weeks of July consistently start FY27 with sharper clarity than those who wait for the tax return to land in September before taking stock.

Full piece on the ProfitPulse blog. Link in the first comment.

#FractionalCFO #AustralianBusiness #SMEFinance
facebook:
EOFY isn't just a compliance deadline. For Australian owner-led businesses, June 30 is also the best moment in the calendar to stop and ask a few commercial questions about the year just closed.

Where did gross margin actually land this year, and do you know why? Which revenue lines carried the year, and which ones quietly dragged? Did the year's profit show up in the bank account?

These questions have answers sitting in the June 30 numbers right now. Most owners just don't have time to look, or no one in their team is paid to.

What's the one number from this year that you'd most like to understand better before FY27 starts?

#AustralianBusiness #SMEFinance
google:
30 June marks the end of the financial year for Australian businesses, and most of the attention this week is on compliance: getting documents to the accountant, finishing reconciliations, making sure the year closes cleanly.

That work is essential. But June 30 is also a useful moment to step back and ask a different kind of question about the year just finished.

At ProfitPulse, the EOFY review we run alongside the compliance process looks at three things: where gross margin landed and whether the business knows why, which revenue lines drove real profitability versus which ones consumed capacity without contributing proportionally, and whether the year's profit translated into cash.

These are not tax questions. They are commercial questions. And the answers shape the decisions that matter in July, August, and September.

For owner-led businesses in Brisbane, the Gold Coast, Sydney, and Melbourne, this kind of year-end commercial debrief is one of the more practical ways to enter FY27 with clarity rather than just a fresh set of invoices and good intentions.

Read the full piece on the ProfitPulse blog.
